text: The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair using the da Vinci XI system, identifying the pars flaccida of the gastro-hepatic ligament. The vessel sealer extend is used to incise the ligament, with dissection carried towards the right cruce. The assistant hand exchanges from holding the pars flaccida to holding adjacent fat, exposing the peritoneal covering on the cruce. The peritoneum over the curl muscle is incised with small bites to expose the muscle fibers, accessing the mediastinum.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal dissection along the length of the cruce, using the assistant hand to retract the hernia sac edge. Blunt spreading in the mediastinum reveals areolar tissue, indicating entry into the avascular plane. The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus while maintaining good retraction and visualization of the right mediastinum. The posterior vagus nerve becomes visible within the fat, and care is taken to preserve it during dissection.

The surgeon focuses on the left cruce, removing the phrenogastric fat pad to expose the edge of the left curl muscle. The anterior vagus nerve is visible in the tissue anterior to the esophagus, and care is taken to preserve it while gently spreading structures within the left mediastinum using the areolar planes.

The surgeon retracts the curl muscle with the left hand to optimize visualization for blunt dissection with the right hand. Attention is turned to the posterior dissection, identifying the posterior confluence of the cruse. The surgeon gently spreads over the left muscle fibers from the right side, pushing mediastinal structures upward with the left hand while ligating posterior attachments with the right. A clear retroesophageal window is visible, and a Penrose drain is passed around the esophagus to aid in retraction.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the esophagus by taking down the remaining mediastinal attachments on the left side, achieving two to three centimeters of intra-abdominal length. The surgeon begins curl closure by protecting the aorta with the left hand while driving a needle through the cruce with the right hand. The camera is adjusted for efficient suturing, stabilizing the loop on the curl muscle to take the next bite. The hiatus is closed with minimal tension, running the suture until the diaphragm approximates the esophagus circumferentially without compression.

The surgeon tightens the final suture bite, releasing tension on the esophagus to allow it to fill the hiatus space completely. The suture is locked by running it back on itself multiple times, completing the hiatal hernia repair.
